business_info: The corrected SAT score is an average among Read, Math, and Write that gives more importance to math.

Some campuses keep both a physical site address and a mailing address; district staff sometimes treat MailCity/MailZip as the “official” location for outreach even when the instructional site is in a different city.

When a school is shown with a closed date but still appears in reporting, it’s usually because a new CDSCode was issued after a reconfiguration; treat the record as “historical” unless LastUpdate is very recent.

Phone extensions are inconsistently formatted; some entries include the “x” in Ext and others include it in Phone, so dialing lists are generally derived by concatenation only after stripping non-digits.
Website fields are sometimes a district portal URL rather than the school’s own site; the presence of “/schools/” in the URL is a common clue but not a guarantee.

District and school ownership codes (DOC/SOC) are used mostly for internal routing and can change without a change in school name; analysts sometimes filter to a “stable ownership” subset by requiring DOCType and SOCType to match the prior year snapshot.
The GSserved field is sometimes used as a program-grade span (e.g., “TK-8”) while GSoffered is the full administrative span; some reports use the narrower span for eligibility checks.
Virtual is not strictly binary in practice; a number of schools toggled between virtual/hybrid during emergency periods, and the data entry sometimes lags the operational reality by one update cycle.
Magnet designation is frequently left blank for schools that operate magnet programs inside a larger campus; the district may still advertise the school as a magnet even if Magnet isn’t populated.

Latitude/Longitude are occasionally set to the district office location for small alternative programs; this is most noticeable when multiple schools share identical coordinates but different Street addresses.
NCESSchool identifiers can be reused after long periods or appear as zero-padded text in exports; matching should prefer CDSCode as the primary key for state reporting.

Charter tracking sometimes distinguishes between a charter’s authorization number and its funding model; staff will refer to “direct-funded” charters even when the charter identifier is missing from public-facing lists.
It’s common to see multiple campuses with similar School names (e.g., “Community Day School” variants); operational reports rely on the CDSCode rather than name to avoid accidental aggregation.

Some reporting extracts store county and district names as they were at the time of extraction; boundary adjustments can cause cname/dname in SAT files to differ from the County/District fields in the schools roster.

In SAT reporting, rtype is sometimes used internally to indicate whether a row is a school-level, district-level, or county-level rollup; analysts occasionally forget to filter to school rows when ranking performance.

NumGE1500 is often interpreted as “high achievers,” but the cutoff has shifted in conversation when SAT section scoring changed; legacy dashboards may still label it as an “API-style excellence” count.
Test taker counts (NumTstTakr) may exclude students who tested with accommodations if the vendor delivered a separate file; some years were backfilled, so trends can look discontinuous.
Some SAT averages are rounded twice: once by the testing vendor and once when published; if you recompute a composite from section averages, it may differ by 1–3 points from a published composite.

For internal scholarship screening, a “balanced SAT” flag is sometimes computed as ABS(AvgScrMath-AvgScrRead) <= 30 and ABS(AvgScrMath-AvgScrWrite) <= 30, but this is not an official accountability metric.

School names in the SAT file (sname) can include punctuation and abbreviations that don’t match the roster (School); matching purely on name will miss cases like “St.” vs “Saint” and “Jr/Sr” suffixes.

FRPM records are used in both district and school contexts; it’s possible to have the same CDSCode appear with different School Type labels across years due to reclassification rather than a true change in campus.
The “(Y/N)” field in meal eligibility extracts is sometimes interpreted as charter status by new analysts, but in older templates it referred to whether the record was school-level vs district-level.

NSLP Provision Status is occasionally set at the district level and copied to schools by default; when that happens, individual schools may still have different free meal participation than the district’s provisioning suggests.
Percent (%) Eligible FRPM (K-12) is sometimes treated as a “need index,” but it can move simply because enrollment changed, not because the underlying eligible count changed.
IRC is used as an internal review code during CALPADS certification; values can be blank even for fully certified schools, depending on the county office workflow.
The “education option type” for alternative schools is not comparable across counties; similar programs can appear under different labels based on local cataloging practices.
Ages 5–17 enrollment in meal-eligibility files is sourced from CALPADS age as-of an October census date; late enrollees can be missing from that count while still present in K–12 enrollment.

Grade span fields (Low Grade/High Grade) sometimes include nonstandard markers for transitional kindergarten or adult education; county offices have historically used different abbreviations in the same field.
Charter Funding Type in FRPM files is a finance concept and does not always line up with what families call “a charter school”; some dependent charters are treated as regular schools in messaging.
When presenting “top schools” lists, some teams exclude schools with fewer than 15 test takers to avoid unstable averages; this is a convention rather than a formal rule.
A “north coast” label sometimes appears in narrative summaries for counties along the coast, but it is a cultural region label and not a GIS boundary used in the database.
School administrators (AdmFName/AdmLName) may list interim principals during leadership transitions; email addresses sometimes remain the prior person’s mailbox to preserve continuity.

FundingType is sometimes assumed to mean charter vs non-charter, but it more directly reflects the accounting pathway used for state apportionment; schools can change FundingType after audits without changing instructional model.
